utterances: Well, hello, Farai.
How are you?
Oh great.
So you - we're taking a look at the big picture here.
We've got Roland Burris waiting to be sworn in later this week as the new junior senator from Illinois.
He is filling President-elect Barack Obama's vacant seat.
And the appointment by impeached Illinois Governor Rod Blagojevich was controversial from the start.
So what's the word online?
Well, overall, there's a tone of suspicion and skepticism, namely over the role Burris's race played in his appointment.
Brandon Whitney of Homeland Colors wrote this on his blog.

Burris is a joke, but the rabid support of people who we don't believe in just because its political expedient is no laughing matter."" And blogger Chris Rabb of Afro-Netizen is even quoted in an Associated Press article saying this.
If there was ever a playing of the race card, this was it.
Blagojevich may be crazy, but he's not stupid.
Well, with President-elect Obama's inauguration less than a week away, what are bloggers saying about that?
Well, another day, another simmering controversy, right?
So...
(Soundbite of laughter)
Yeah.
The head of the Alabama NAACP says there's an aspect of Obama's inaugural parade that he finds offensive.
And it's the traditional inclusion of the trail maids, it's these women who wears those big pastel hoop skirts with the bonnets and the umbrellas.
He says those women in those dresses remind him of slavery, so.
Danielle Belton of Black Snob writes, ""I'm sorry, I wasn't aware that we black people ran out of real problems and only had mock outrage to deal out.
(Soundbite of laughter)
All right.
I also understand a few bloggers are taking preemptive measures just in case people get too carried away with their Obama worship next week.
Right.
And Michael Fauntroy, on his blog, is tackling this talk of an Obama presidency ushering in this wave of post-racial American-ness.
So he, you know, he debunks all that.

And then blogger Corey Richardson of Vexed In The City actually called in to read us some of what he wrote in a post called ""An Open Letter to Black People Regarding Barack Obama/Jesus."" So here's part of that.

Just so you know, Barack Obama doesn't have time to care about the following black issues: Reparations, the NBA, police brutality, teen pregnancy, what happened to UPN, dog fighting, whether or not to put big momma in a home, pardoning your favorite rapper who just might be in jail - side note, keep your head up, Cool C - whether or not, universal health care will cover the Sugar or the William Sisters.
So there you have it.
Barack Obama does not care about the William Sisters.
(Soundbite of laughter)
Hmm, man, that's - I don't know.
He's going to get some pushback on that one.
He is going to get some pushback on that one.
Well, Geoff, as always, great.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Geoffrey Bennett is the digital producer for News & Notes, he joined me from the studios of NPR West.
I'm Farai Chideya, and this is News & Notes.
In Somalia and Haiti, residents have taken to the streets in protest of food prices.
It's just part of a global problem.
A few have even died for the cause.
We see the plight on the news, but what does it feel like when those people are your people?
What do Americans of African and Caribbean descent do when they see suffering in the countries that they or their parents hail from?
Well, today we're speaking with Marjorie Valbrun.
She's a Haitian-American living in Washington, D.C., and she's written about the issue for the online magazine TheRoot.com.
Hey, Marjorie.
Hello.
(Soundbite of opera music)
Well, let's get back into that potential influence.
How could U.S.
- those levers be used?
Well, there's a whole variety of different ways they can be used, and people often talk in the very stark terms about, you know, well, the United States should simply cut off all aid to Israel.
I think that in most cases that's too much; that's sort of too harsh a response to many issues of difference.

The way to think about this, it seems to me, is about the United States simply having a more normal relationship with Israel, instead of a special relationship, one where - when they were doing things that we thought were in our interest and in their interest, that we would support them, but when they were doing things that the United States was opposed to - like, say, building settlements in the West Bank - the United States would be able to distance itself from Israel, might be able to reduce some of that support or make it conditional, and also simply have a much more candid conversation, both in public and in private, with what American policy is and what American support was going to be.
Special relationship, the United States enjoys special relationships with two countries: Britain and with Israel.
That's right, although in the case of Britain, first of all, it wasn't always that way.

We had a not-very-close relationship throughout much of the 19th century, and the special relationship doesn't involve the United States giving, you know, large amounts of direct economic and military aid and giving that unconditionally.
U.S.
aid to Israel is about $500 per year for every Israeli; plus we give them consistent diplomatic and military support.

Again, there - you can defend that in a variety of ways, but the big problem is that the aid tends to be given unconditionally, as I said before, even when Israel is doing things that aren't good for us and ultimately aren't good for them.
Where do Israel's - are Israel's interests and American interests congruent?
Do they - are there - in some cases they're clearly the same, but in other cases, do they divide?
Well, the most obvious one is the settlement policy.

I mean, many Israelis will tell you that the entire effort to colonize the West Bank with a series of settlements over the last 40 to 50 years was a strategic disaster, a strategic error, of really quite enormous proportions, even leading Israeli Prime Minister Ehud Olmert now to question the whole enterprise as threatening the long-term future of the Jewish state.
The United States would have been a better friend to Israel if it had consistently opposed that and used the leverage at its disposal to try and bring an end to the settlement process.
That would make a two-state solution easier now.
That would have, I think, made it less likely the groups like Hamas would have achieved the popularity that they have achieved.
It would have been better for us, better for Israel; ultimately, I think, better for the Palestinians as well.

But we didn't do that because, again, we have a special relationship, and it's politically very difficult for American politicians to take a more independent stance, even though this is an issue where the Israeli government's policies and American interests are really at odds.
And let's bring another voice into the conversation.

Joining us here in Studio 3A is Aaron David Miller, author of ""The Much Too Promised Land."" He's been a Middle East adviser to six secretaries of State from both Republican and Democratic administrations, now a public-policy fellow at the Woodrow Wilson International Center for Scholars here in Washington.
It's nice to have you back on the program.
Pleasure to be here, Neal.
And when we talk about U.S.
influence over Israel, you've been in the position of trying to actually flip those levers from time to time.
How easy is it?
It's very difficult.
It's difficult because of domestic politics, for sure.

It's difficult because America's relationship with a close ally, and Israel is a close ally, is a complex one, and you never want to humiliate or, if at all possible, pressure or sanction an ally, lest you weaken your own policies and undermine your general approach.
And then there's the relationship between presidents and Israeli prime ministers, which tend to be very close.
American presidents end up being in the thrall of Israeli prime ministers.
I talk about the notion of the Jewish lobby of one, which is the impact that an Israeli prime minister has on an American president.
That's, frankly, much more consequential than the vaunted, you know, pro-Israeli lobby which constrains American policy.
So, it's complex.

My basic argument is that when a willful president, in the service of American national interest, in discreet circumstances, when pressure essentially can actually produce something, American presidents can be plenty tough.
I use Nixon and Kissinger, Jimmy Carter and George H.
W.

Bush and Jim Baker as three examples of situations where American pressure defied not only domestic political pressures, but in the service of American diplomacy and also in the service of Israeli and American interests, actually succeeded.
We were talking with Stephen Walt about the special relationship.
He believes it should be a more normal relationship.
Well, that's not my view.
I think that it shouldn't be exclusive.

I mean, the special relationship with Israel is driven by shared values, and I know shared values is considered to be a soft and somewhat idealized concept in foreign policy among realists, but the fact is shared values is extremely important.
We want to promote and support societies that share our values because there are too few of them.
What we can't allow to have happen and which - and what I'm afraid has happened for 16 years now, eight under Bill Clinton and eight under George W.
Bush, is that the special relationship has gotten out of control; it's become exclusive.

We don't criticize the Israelis for things they do; we road-test every idea with them before we show it to the other side; and we tend to acquiesce and agree on Israeli ideas, like Camp David, for example, of July in 2000, which was not sufficiently prepared.
So, as a consequence of all this, we are empathetic and reassuring, but we're not tough enough.
And my advice to Barack Obama, unsolicited, of course, is that you be tough, smart and fair; if you're that, you may actually get in the game.
Let's get some listeners involved in the conversation.
Our guests are Stephen Walt and Aaron David Miller.
We're talking about U.S.
leverage on Israel, what it is and how to use it.
800-989-8255; email talk@npr.org.
We'll begin with Kevin, Kevin with us from Fall River, Massachusetts.
Hello, yes.

I think that we should possibly provide grants instead of loans that we then forgive and, therefore, have a mechanism by now of not - you know, eliminating any condition on those loans, And they're not truly loans if they're forgiven later.

And the other issue is that I'm very, very pro-Israel and that the best thing Israel needs is an autonomous, independent United States that can be - work with Israel as a good-cop/bad-cop deal: put pressure on Israel and make it possible for Israeli politicians to do the right thing, to end the occupation, and not militarize - attempt to use military means almost exclusively to try to resolve their security problems.
Not unlike the neo-conservatives, you know, they said they did the Iraq war because of America, you know, for the United States.
But that's not the case.
It's similar that - what Israel's more right-wing militaristic policy has done.
They say that to defend Israel, but in reality, in the long term, its undermined Israeli security.
So, we need an autonomous, independent United States that can call Israel into question for its own sake as well as ours.
Well, let me ask, Aaron David Miller, about the first element of what Kevin's talking about.
What are the components of U.S.
aid to Israel?
How is it supplied?
And I know a billion of it comes under the treaty from Camp David, the agreement with Egypt.
Yeah.
I mean, economic assistance has relatively - has more or less zeroed out.
It's really a question military assistance and help we provide with research and development for weapons.
Israel is a non-NATO ally as well, and they derive benefits from that.
You know, on issue of American independence, I do believe that if America is to be a credible, effective mediator, it does need a certain measure of independence with respect to Israeli policy.
Otherwise, how can we truly be viewed as effective?
And by the way, we're not an honest broker.
I mean, I've long ago surrendered that notion.
We'll always be closer to Israel for the reasons that I laid out.
The real question is, can we be an effective broker?
And when we're tough, smart and fair with Israelis and the Arabs, I believe we can.
Honest brokers, Stephen Walt?
Quickly.
There have been a few moments in the past, not in the recent past, where the United States has acted a bit more like an honest broker.
And Aaron referred to, for example, President Bush number 41, who did - you know, at one point made loan guarantees conditional on an Israeli halt of the settlements.
The problem there, of course, is that the pressure tends not to be sustained for very long.
And under the whole entire Oslo process, the United States continued its aid and Israel continued to build settlements.
I mean, I think that - you know, I can't disagree with him the idea of being tough, smart and fair.
The question is whether or not President Obama is going to be able to sustain the kind of dialogue and the kind of pressure that's going to be necessary to get the situation to change.
Kevin, thanks very much for the call.
